title: SEGMENT 8 — How cancer travels: angiogenesis, EMT, and metastasis

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — How cancer travels: angiogenesis, EMT, and metastasis
A cancer that stays in one place is rarely what kills you.
A bad lump in the breast, caught early, is usually curable by surgery alone.
What turns cancer into a fatal disease is when it travels — when cells from the original tumor escape, ride the bloodstream or the lymphatic vessels to distant organs, and set up new colonies.
That process is called metastasis, and roughly nine out of ten cancer deaths are caused by metastasis rather than the original tumor.
So understanding how cancer travels is, in a real sense, understanding what kills people.
Three big capabilities have to come together.
Let's take them in order.
Capability one: feeding the tumor.
I mentioned earlier that a tumor bigger than about a sesame seed can't survive on diffusion alone.
The cells in the middle would suffocate.
So a growing tumor has to recruit a blood supply.
This is angiogenesis — the growth of new blood vessels — and it's a normal process that the body uses, for instance, in wound healing.
Tumors hijack it.
The main signal a tumor sends out to recruit blood vessels is a protein called VEGF — vascular endothelial growth factor.
VEGF drifts out of the tumor, reaches nearby blood vessels, and tells the cells of those vessels to sprout, divide, and reach toward the source of the signal.
New capillaries grow into the tumor.
The catch — and this matters therapeutically — is that tumor-built blood vessels are often a mess.
They're leaky, disorganized, twisted, like a junior city planner with no oversight ran the water pipes through a slum.
So tumor blood supply is often poor quality, which means parts of the tumor live in low oxygen, which has consequences we'll come back to.
We've built drugs against VEGF.
Bevacizumab is an antibody that grabs VEGF out of the extracellular space before it can reach blood vessels.
Other drugs called VEGFR inhibitors block the receptor on the vessel side.
These drugs slow tumor growth by starving the tumor of its blood supply.
They don't cure cancer alone, but they're useful additions to many regimens.
Capability two: leaving home.
A normal cell, especially an epithelial cell — the kind that lines surfaces and forms most solid tumors — is glued to its neighbors and to its underlying scaffolding.
Cells are designed to stay put.
To metastasize, a cancer cell has to dissolve those connections and acquire the ability to crawl.
This transformation has a name: the epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, or EMT.
The analogy I like: imagine the cells of an epithelial sheet as bricks in a wall, held together by mortar.
EMT is what happens when a brick dissolves its own mortar, sprouts legs, and walks away.
The key molecular marker is loss of a protein called E-cadherin, which is the main "glue" molecule between epithelial cells.
Cancer cells silence E-cadherin, lose their attachments, and become motile and invasive.
Several master-regulator transcription factors — SNAIL, TWIST, ZEB — orchestrate this transition.
Capability three: finding a home far away.

The migrating cell has to break through the basement membrane underneath the epithelial layer (it does this by secreting enzymes that chew up the protein meshwork — matrix metalloproteinases, or MMPs), enter a blood or lymph vessel, survive the trip through the circulation (most cells don't — circulation is hostile, fast, full of sheer forces and immune cells), exit the vessel somewhere new, and start growing in a foreign tissue.
That last step is the hardest.
And it leads to one of the most elegant ideas in cancer biology, proposed by a Victorian English surgeon named Stephen Paget in 1889 — well before anyone knew what cancer cells were made of.
Paget noticed that different cancers metastasize to different organs in highly non-random patterns.
Breast cancer loves bone, liver, lung, and brain.
Prostate cancer loves bone.
Eye melanoma loves the liver.
Why these patterns?
Paget proposed what's now called the seed and soil hypothesis.
The cancer cells, he said, are seeds; the distant organs are soils; and not every seed grows in every soil.
A seed has to find a tissue whose growth factors, extracellular matrix, oxygen tension, and resident cells are compatible.
A breast cancer cell can survive being scattered to many places, but it can only build a colony where it finds friendly soil.
The seed-and-soil framing is over a hundred and thirty years old and we keep finding it correct in molecular detail.

Modern research has even shown that the primary tumor can prepare distant soil in advance — sending out signals that prime certain organs to be welcoming to its seeds before any cell has even arrived.
It's called the pre-metastatic niche, and it's eerie.
The tumor scouts the land.
So that's the migration story: feed the tumor with new blood vessels, transition to a mobile state through EMT, ride the circulation, and find the right soil to take root.
Each step is, in principle, a therapeutic target — but metastasis remains the hardest part of cancer to treat, because by the time it's detected, the seeds are usually already widely scattered.
